metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a negative-existence proving command (e.g. `grep -rn 'hasattr' test_applier_exceptions.py test_fs_tools_extraction.py test_overlap_retrieve.py | grep -v '# allowed'` exits non-zero if any hasattr survives) alongside `make test`, so the AC cannot be satisfied by the unchanged code.
findings.finding: `make test` is the sole proving command, but the existing hasattr tests already pass `make test`; a green test suite does not prove the anti-pattern replacement occurred. No command verifies that hasattr/constant-existence patterns are absent from the five named files.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Running `grep -En "hasattr|== 75|_FIELD_WEIGHTS" tests/test_applier_exceptions.py tests/test_applier_reschedule.py tests/test_fs_tools_extraction.py tests/test_overlap_retrieve.py tests/test_code_review_artifact.py` returns no matches.' This pairs with `make test` to prove both that new tests pass AND old patterns are removed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the identity/reuse seam used to replace the 7-symbol hasattr sweep covers all 7 symbols — either extend test_pai_tools_consume_the_shared_cluster to assert identity for _git_tracked, _NOISE_DIRS, and _NOISE_SUFFIXES, or name an alternative behavioral seam that exercises each of those three.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'prove the fs_tools cluster via the existing identity/reuse seam,' implying a full replacement of all 7 hasattr checks is achievable through the seam that already exists. However, the existing identity seam (test_pai_tools_consume_the_shared_cluster) only covers 4 of the 7 symbols (_safe_path, _discovery_filter, _within_root, _SCAN_MAX_FILES). The 3 remaining symbols from the hasattr sweep (_git_tracked, _NOISE_DIRS, _NOISE_SUFFIXES) have no existing identity or behavioral seam in the repo — no test confirms pai_tools (or any consumer) re-uses them by identity.
findings.location: What section / AC: 'fs_tools reuse is proven by identity, not existence' + 'test_fs_tools_extraction.py::test_fs_tools_exposes_the_shared_cluster (7-symbol hasattr sweep)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend test_pai_tools_consume_the_shared_cluster (or the replacement test) with: `assert pai_tools._git_tracked is fs_tools._git_tracked`, `assert pai_tools._NOISE_DIRS is fs_tools._NOISE_DIRS`, `assert pai_tools._NOISE_SUFFIXES is fs_tools._NOISE_SUFFIXES`. If pai_tools does not re-export those 3 symbols, name the actual consumer that does and test identity there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly verifies the old anti-pattern assertions (hasattr sweeps, `==75` constant check, private `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` value comparison, source-grep assertion) are removed from each named file.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'Replace' (implying removal of the old anti-pattern tests) but no AC criterion verifies that the hasattr/constant-existence tests are actually deleted. An implementer could add new behavioral tests alongside the old ones and pass all five AC bullets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] The original hasattr-only, constant-value, and source-inspection assertions are deleted from all five named test files (no hasattr calls or raw constant comparisons remain for the covered behaviors).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable for the `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` AC: name the ranking inputs (e.g. two candidate documents differing on one weighted field) and the asserted output (e.g. the higher-weighted-field document ranks first), so the test design is unambiguous.
findings.finding: 'exercise ranking that `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` drives' does not specify what inputs to supply, what ranking output to observe, or what assertion to make — leaving the entire test design undefined for this AC.
findings.location: What section — '_FIELD_WEIGHTS' bullet; AC2 — '_FIELD_WEIGHTS are asserted via observable behavior, not constant values'

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact seam for the code_review Jira-exclusion test: name either the function to call directly (e.g. `compute_outbound_mutations` from `outbound_differ.py` via importlib-by-path with a minimal stub corpus) or confirm a subprocess invocation is acceptable, and document the sys.modules keys that must be pre-seeded to avoid credential/isolation failures.
findings.finding: The plan says 'run reconcile and assert code_review tickets are excluded from Jira sync' but does not specify which seam to invoke. The reconciler runs as a subprocess-isolated, stdlib-only program loaded via importlib-by-path; a full subprocess run requires live Jira credentials, while a direct call to compute_outbound_mutations/outbound_differ requires navigating the importlib-by-path loading pattern (sys.modules key aliasing, _load_config() delegation). Without naming the seam, an implementer may attempt a full subprocess invocation (hitting credential failures) or a naive import (breaking the module-isolation contract). The mechanism as stated is incomplete for this case.
findings.location: AC: 'code_review Jira exclusion is proven by running reconcile, not source grep' / What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'Call `compute_outbound_mutations(local_tickets=[code_review_ticket], ...)` loaded via the same importlib-by-path pattern as other reconciler tests (pre-seeding `rebar_reconciler.config` in sys.modules), passing an empty Jira snapshot; assert the returned mutations list is empty for the code_review ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify all five target file paths with their full repo-relative paths (e.g., `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_exceptions.py`) in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan names test_applier_exceptions.py and test_applier_reschedule.py without full paths; both actually live under tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/, not tests/unit/. An implementer scanning tests/unit/ directly will not find them, causing confusion about scope.
findings.location: Scope section / Why section — file list
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the short names in Scope with:
- `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_exceptions.py`
- `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_reschedule.py`
- `tests/unit/test_fs_tools_extraction.py`
- `tests/unit/test_overlap_retrieve.py`
- `tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether test_fs_tools_exposes_the_shared_cluster should be deleted (relying on the existing identity test) or converted to behavioral assertions, and confirm that test_pai_tools_consume_the_shared_cluster already satisfies the AC.
findings.finding: The AC 'fs_tools reuse is proven by identity, not existence' is already partially satisfied by the existing test_pai_tools_consume_the_shared_cluster test (identity assertions with `is` on lines 33-37 of test_fs_tools_extraction.py). The plan does not acknowledge this, leaving it unclear whether test_fs_tools_exposes_the_shared_cluster's hasattr sweep should be deleted or converted, and whether the existing identity test is the intended replacement or must also be extended.
findings.location: AC: 'fs_tools reuse is proven by identity, not existence.' / tests/unit/test_fs_tools_extraction.py lines 29-37

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific object or fixture whose identity should be asserted (e.g. 'assert that calling `get_shared_cluster()` twice returns the same object via `is`') rather than referring to an unnamed 'seam'.
findings.finding: 'prove the fs_tools cluster via the existing identity/reuse seam' names a 'seam' without identifying it — the specific fixture, function, or object reference to use for the identity assertion is not described.
findings.location: What section — 'fs_tools cluster via the existing identity/reuse seam'; AC4 — 'fs_tools reuse is proven by identity, not existence'
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: 'Assert `get_shared_cluster() is get_shared_cluster()` (or the equivalent singleton accessor used in production code), confirming the cluster object is reused rather than recreated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the Why or What section explaining why behavioral provocation (triggering real code paths) is chosen — e.g. 'because existence tests allow behavior to be silently broken while tests remain green; only exercising the actual path proves both presence and correctness.'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why behavioral provocation (triggering real paths) is the chosen approach over alternatives such as type/protocol-level contracts (e.g. asserting exception class hierarchy and raise-site decorators) or integration-boundary contract tests. The absence of a stated rationale leaves the implementer without guidance on where to draw the behavioral line, and makes the approach's tradeoffs (e.g. needing stubs/mocks for reconciler isolation) invisible.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
